experiences.intentions: Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; the other three are unknown good roles. As evil leader on the first 2-person quest, optimal play is to propose a plausible-looking team that includes one evil. Sending myself with an unknown is standard and gives us control over the quest while not exposing the full evil pair. Revealing a team with both evils would be too suspicious if it failed, and excluding all evils gives good a free pass. Picking myself and a random good candidate is best.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 1 is Evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes a known Evil on quest 1, so from Good's perspective this should be rejected to avoid giving Evil control and to signal suspicion without overexposing exact knowledge. In team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing known Evil when possible.
Player 3: No concrete role reads yet from a single opening proposal. As a good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-player team is standard because it gives early information without much downside, and rejecting blindly can waste rounds. My vote reveals little beyond a willingness to test the first team, which is generally pro-good.
Player 1: I know only player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are unknown good roles. The phase is team voting on quest 1, team size 2, with proposed team [0,1] that I myself proposed. As evil on a 2-person first quest, being on the team gives me a chance to fail immediately and create pressure, and approving my own team is consistent. My vote reveals little since leaders often approve their own proposals; rejecting my own team would look strange and needlessly distance me from a strong sabotage opportunity.
Player 2: No concrete role information yet from quest results or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1, a 2-player team of [0,1] is standard and could easily be all-good; rejecting gives little value this early and risks unnecessary failed votes. Approving reveals little and helps advance the game to gather quest information.
Player 4: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 1. Proposed team [0,1] includes my teammate but not me. On round 1, approving a team with one evil can secure an early fail while appearing reasonable; rejecting would draw unnecessary attention to me and risks losing a good sabotage opportunity. My vote reveals little because many players approve round-1 teams.

Player 1: I know player 4 is evil with me; others are unknown. This is Quest 1 with a 2-person team [0,1], and I am a Minion on the team. Early in 5-player Avalon, failing immediately can create suspicion on me and player 0, especially since I proposed the team. Passing preserves cover for me and my teammate while giving little hard info, which generally helps evil more long-term than an obvious early sabotage. My action reveals that at least one evil on team may have chosen to hide, which keeps identities obscured and benefits evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes known evil player 1, so it is highly likely to risk a fail on quest 2. As good, optimal play is to reject teams containing known evil when possible. My vote reveals little individually and helps steer away from a bad team.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [1,0] succeeded, so 0 and 1 look somewhat good but not confirmed since evil can pass early. Proposed team [0,1,2] keeps both prior-success members and adds one new player, which is a standard strong team for Quest 2. As a Servant, I want to approve reasonable information-gathering teams that include successful prior members rather than reject without cause. My vote reveals trust in the best available evidence and helps good avoid unnecessary rejection cycles.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. The first quest team [1,0] succeeded with 0 fails, which is consistent with 1 choosing to pass to build trust. Now the proposed team [0,1,2] again includes my teammate but not me. As evil, I generally want to avoid approving teams that can succeed without my control and that further clear Player 1 if they pass again. Rejecting also avoids helping a likely-good-heavy team and keeps options open for a future team including me. My vote itself reveals limited info; rejecting here is fine even if some good players also reject on suspicion.
Player 2: Quest 1 team [1,0] succeeded, so 0 and 1 each look somewhat better than random, though one could still be evil and choose to pass. As a good Servant on quest 2, my goal is to back teams with the strongest available evidence. Team [0,1,2] adds me to the previously successful pair, which is a very reasonable low-risk progression team. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with trusting a successful prior team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: I know Player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are good. Quest 1 succeeded on team [1,0], which is consistent with me passing to avoid suspicion. Current phase is team voting on [0,1,2]. As evil, I want to balance sabotage with cover. Approving keeps me on the team again and gives evil a chance to fail quest 2 while preserving my good image if needed. Rejecting would look odd after being on the successful first quest and could slow momentum without benefit. My vote itself reveals little, and approving is best for evil here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 1 is Evil. Proposed team [0,1] includes a known Evil, so as Good I should reject to avoid giving Evil another quest slot. The past two successes with player 1 likely mean they chose to pass to stay hidden; approving would help Evil more than the small informational value gained.
Player 3: Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [1,0] and [0,1,2], so players 0 and 1 look strongly good; player 2 is somewhat cleared by quest 2 succeeding. As a good Servant in team voting on quest 3, approving a team of the two most trusted players is optimal, especially since a 2-player team can still succeed and gives little room for evil. Approving reveals consistency with backing successful cores and does not harm good.
Player 2: Players 0 and 1 were on both successful quests, so they look relatively trustworthy. My own quest team with 0,1,2 also succeeded, which gives no reason to suspect 2 either from my perspective. This proposed team [0,1] repeats the proven successful pair on quest 3, where a success is highly valuable for Good. As a Servant, optimal play is to approve a team with the strongest success evidence. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with backing proven-good teams, which helps Good more than it hurts.
Player 1: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0 and 1 have been on both successful quests, but as evil I know at least one of those successes came from me choosing to pass, so their records are not fully clearing. Current phase is team voting on a 2-player team [0,1] for quest 3, which requires only one fail in 5-player Avalon, so if this team passes and includes me, I could fail and score evil a point. Approving helps put me on the quest and keeps suspicion low since I was on prior successes; rejecting risks losing a strong sabotage chance and may look odd.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Both prior successful quests included [0,1] and [0,1,2], meaning 1 chose to pass twice to avoid early suspicion, so 0 and 2 look relatively good to others. Current phase is team voting on Quest 3 with proposed team [0,1]. As evil, I want to prevent another clean success and avoid endorsing a small team centered on my teammate 1, which can attract scrutiny after repeated inclusions. Rejecting is standard evil play here because we are down 0-2 and need chances to reshape teams or create suspicion. My vote itself reveals little since rejecting a third team with recurring 1 is defensible for either side.
